---
title: "Modelling options for reporting delays"
subtitle: "InSight Net nowcasting strike team"
author: "Sam Abbott"
date: "2026-04-26"
format:
  revealjs:
    output: modelling-options-slides.html
    footer: "Modelling options for reporting delays"
    slide-level: 2
    chalkboard: true
    incremental: false
engine: knitr
---

## From three examples to a methods view

You have just seen three concrete cases:

- Massachusetts (respiratory ED visits via NSSP)
- Washington State (case investigation, COVID-19)
- South Carolina (measles)

Different surveillance streams, different operational responses, but a similar underlying data picture.

## Right truncation, and more {.smaller}

![](figures/truncation_final.png){fig-align="center" width="50%"}

All three examples share *right truncation*: most events have happened, but some are not yet visible to us.

They also have other things going on — data revisions, sites coming and going, missing or messy fields — that need different kinds of methods.

::: {.aside}
Figure from the [NFIDD short course](https://nfidd.github.io/sismid/) (Abbott, Funk, Lison, Bracher, Bosse).
:::

## Modelling for delays is broader than nowcasting alone {.smaller}

```{tikz}
%%| fig-align: center
%%| fig-ext: png
%%| fig-width: 8.5
%%| fig-height: 3.4
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  every node/.style={align=center, font=\footnotesize},
  stage/.style={draw, rounded corners, minimum height=8mm,
                minimum width=24mm, fill=blue!8},
  method/.style={draw, dashed, rounded corners, minimum height=9mm,
                 minimum width=27mm, fill=yellow!18},
  arr/.style={->, thick, >=latex},
  marr/.style={->, dashed, gray, >=latex}
]
  \node[stage] (a) at (0,0) {Events occur};
  \node[stage] (b) at (3.1,0) {Events reported};
  \node[stage] (c) at (6.2,0) {Partial observations};
  \node[stage] (d) at (9.3,0) {Decisions};

  \draw[arr] (a) -- node[above, font=\scriptsize\itshape]{delay} (b);
  \draw[arr] (b) -- (c);
  \draw[arr] (c) -- (d);

  \node[method] (m1) at (0,-1.8) {Delay distribution\\estimation};
  \node[method] (m2) at (3.1,-1.8) {Modelling the\\reporting process};
  \node[method] (m3) at (6.2,-1.8) {Nowcasting\\(joint or two-step)};
  \node[method] (m4) at (9.3,-1.8) {Forecasting with\\reporting delays};

  \draw[marr] (m1) -- (a);
  \draw[marr] (m2) -- (b);
  \draw[marr] (m3) -- (c);
  \draw[marr] (m4) -- (d);
\end{tikzpicture}
```

Each method attaches at a different point in the same pipeline; the right choice depends on the question, not the method.

## Modelling by challenge {.smaller}

| Challenge | Approaches | What you get |
|---|---|---|
| **Reporting delays** (correcting delayed data) | Simple (pruning, caveats, report-date), multiplicative (chain ladder), regression, generative (Bayesian, mechanistic), other statistical (ML) | Estimates of current levels and trends with uncertainty |
| **Data revisions** | Model the revision process or use latest snapshot only | Stable estimates that do not flip with each new release |
| **Site drop-in / drop-out** | Pooling across sites; explicit reporting-population models | Jurisdiction-wide estimates from changing site sets |
| **Other data quality** | Pre-processing, deduplication, format harmonisation | Cleaner inputs upstream of any of the above |

: {.striped .hover tbl-colwidths="[18,52,30]"}

More detail in the guide's [modelling options](../methods/index.qmd) section.

## Up next: rapid talks on modelling

You will hear participants describe their own models and tools.

As you listen, three things to note:

- **What data does each method need?** Line list, snapshots, full reporting triangle?
- **What does it give back?** Point estimates, intervals, mechanistic structure?
- **Where does it attach in the pipeline above?**

The group exercise after will work on how to choose between these.

## Our tools and courses {.smaller}

Most of the methods we work on sit in the [epinowcast](https://www.epinowcast.org/) community:

- [epinowcast](https://package.epinowcast.org/), [epidist](https://epidist.epinowcast.org/), [primarycensored](https://primarycensored.epinowcast.org/), [baselinenowcast](https://baselinenowcast.epinowcast.org/) — R packages for nowcasting, delay estimation, and primary event censored distributions
- [EpiNow2](https://epiforecasts.io/EpiNow2/) — R package for Bayesian Rt and forecasting with reporting delays
- The [EpiAware](https://github.com/EpiAware) org for the same ideas in Julia, including [CensoredDistributions.jl](https://github.com/EpiAware/CensoredDistributions.jl)

We also teach a free open-source short course on these topics:

- [NFIDD](https://nfidd.github.io/nfidd/) — *Nowcasting and forecasting of infectious disease dynamics*, available online any time
- Taught in person at [SISMID 2026](https://sph.emory.edu/SISMID/) (Emory, July 2026): [course site](https://nfidd.github.io/sismid/)

If any of these are relevant to your setting, please grab me after the session.

[Back to the session overview](index.qmd)
